title: A Guide to Transfer Pricing for Small International Businesses

author.name: Elena Petrova
author.bio: An international business lawyer by trade, Elena breaks down the complexities of freelance contracts, corporate structures, and international liability. Her goal is to empower freelancers with the legal knowledge to operate confidently.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Formalize the relationship between your companies with a signed Intercompany Agreement that specifies services, pricing methodology, and payment terms.
Price all intercompany transactions using the 'arm's length principle,' ensuring the rate is what you would charge an unrelated third-party client.
Create a defensible paper trail by consistently issuing detailed invoices and transferring actual funds between your entities for all services rendered.
Maintain a real-time compliance file containing your agreement, invoices, proof of payment, and a simple memo explaining your pricing to defend against audits.
summary: Solopreneurs with multi-entity global businesses risk double taxation and severe fines if they fail to properly price transactions between their own companies. The core advice is to adhere to the "arm's length principle" by creating a formal intercompany agreement, executing professional invoices for all internal services, and maintaining a simple documentation file to justify your pricing. This disciplined approach protects the liability and tax advantages of your business structure, transforming a complex compliance requirement into a strong defense against costly audits.
ai_overview_answer: For a solopreneur with a multi-entity business, transfer pricing is the price one of their companies charges the other for services, goods, or intellectual property. All internal transactions must adhere to the "arm's length principle," meaning the price must be what two independent companies would agree upon in the open market. To comply, you must first create a formal Intercompany Agreement defining the commercial relationship, services, and pricing methodology. Following this, you must execute the agreement by issuing professional invoices and ensuring actual funds are transferred between your companies for any work performed. Finally, maintain a compliance file with the agreement, invoices, proof of payment, and a simple memo explaining how your pricing was determined. This process is critical for proving your entities are legitimately separate and avoiding severe penalties like double taxation and audits.

@graph.headline: A Guide to Transfer Pricing for Small International Businesses
@graph.description: A simple 3-step playbook for solopreneurs to manage transfer pricing, create intercompany agreements, and build a defensible compliance file to protect their global business structure.

step.name: The Foundation – Your Intercompany Agreement
step.text: Your first and most powerful tool is a formal contract between your two companies: the Intercompany Agreement (IA). Think of this not as a bureaucratic chore, but as the constitution for your global business. It proves to tax authorities that your internal dealings are deliberate, professional, and governed by a clear commercial framework.

step.name: The Execution – Invoicing and Payments
step.text: An agreement is a statement of intent; the invoice and payment are the tangible proof. This step is about bringing your IA to life through consistent, professional execution, creating a clean paper trail that any tax authority can follow.

step.name: The Defense – Your Contemporaneous Documentation
step.text: Many entrepreneurs fear they need a $20,000 formal transfer pricing study. For a solo-operated business, this is usually unnecessary. Your goal is to create contemporaneous documentation—a simple, organized file you build in real-time to prove you’ve made a reasonable, good-faith effort to comply. This file is your first and best line of defense.

mainEntity.name: Do I need transfer pricing if I'm the only employee?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, absolutely. The rules apply to transactions between legally separate entities, regardless of employee count. When you formed two companies, you created two distinct legal "persons." The law requires them to transact with each other as if they were unrelated to maintain the integrity of your liability shield.

mainEntity.name: What is a simple example of transfer pricing for a consultant?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your US LLC holds your brand and intellectual property. Your UK Ltd signs a client in London. The UK Ltd, which receives the client payment, must pay the US LLC a fee (e.g., a percentage of revenue) for the right to use its brand and methodologies to service that client. This payment is the transfer price, documented with an invoice from the US to the UK entity.

mainEntity.name: How do I document transfer pricing without a $20,000 study?

acceptedAnswer.text: You build the "good-enough" compliance file detailed in Step 3. For a solo-run business, the priority is creating contemporaneous documentation—evidence that you established a fair price at the time of the transaction. Your self-built file containing the agreement, invoices, payment proofs, and a pricing memo achieves this.

mainEntity.name: What is the arm's length principle in simple terms?

acceptedAnswer.text: It means the price for a transaction between your own companies must be the same as it would be if they were completely unrelated. The test is: "What would I pay an independent third party for this service?" That price ensures profits are taxed fairly in the countries where the value was actually created.

It is a formal contract between your two related companies that defines the commercial relationship. It outlines the specific services, the pricing methodology, and payment terms. Having this contract proves your internal dealings are not arbitrary but are governed by a clear, pre-defined framework—a critical element of robust compliance.

body.content: The Solopreneur's Guide to Transfer Pricing: Protecting Your Global Business Structure

You built your business with a sophisticated, multi-entity structure for a reason. Whether it’s a US LLC paired with a UK Ltd or a similar cross-border setup, you created it to gain a liability shield, unlock new markets, and operate with tax efficiency. But this powerful architecture rests on a single, critical discipline: treating your companies as genuinely separate entities.
This is where transfer pricing comes in.

Forget the intimidating corporate jargon. At its core, **transfer pricing** is simply the price one of your companies charges your *other* company for goods, services, or intellectual property. If your US entity develops a strategy that your UK entity uses to win a client, the UK company must "pay" the US one for that work. This internal transaction, while between related parties, must be handled with the same commercial logic as a deal between strangers.

The entire system rests on one golden rule: the **arm's length principle**. It mandates that the price you set between your own companies must be the same as if they were two independent businesses negotiating in the open market. The simplest test is to ask: *What would I pay a third-party firm for this same service?* That is your arm's length price. This principle ensures profits are taxed where value is created, preventing the artificial shifting of income to lower-tax jurisdictions.
Mastering this isn't just about compliance; it's about protecting the very advantages you worked so hard to build.
The High Stakes: Why Getting This Wrong Is Not an Option
For a solopreneur, the consequences of mismanaging transfer pricing are disproportionately severe. The risks aren't abstract; they are concrete threats that can destabilize your business.

items: **The Nightmare of Double Taxation:** This is the most painful penalty. If one country’s tax authority decides your UK Ltd underpaid your US LLC, they will adjust your UK income upwards and tax you on it. The problem? The other country has no obligation to grant a refund for the tax you already paid on that same income. The result is paying tax twice on the exact same profit, decimating your margins.

**Crippling Financial Penalties:** Tax authorities view improper transfer pricing as a serious offense. In jurisdictions like the United States, penalties can range from 20% to 40% of the underpaid tax. For a solo professional, a penalty of that magnitude could erase an entire year's profit.

**The Crushing Weight of an Audit:** A tax dispute is a grueling, prolonged affair that drains your most valuable resources: time, focus, and energy. Once flagged for non-compliance, you can expect heightened scrutiny for years to come. The stress and distraction can be as damaging as the financial cost itself.

body.content: A 3-Step Playbook for Bulletproof Transfer Pricing

These risks are serious, but they are entirely manageable. By adopting a disciplined, proactive approach, you can transform transfer pricing from a source of anxiety into a fortress for your business. This simple, three-step playbook is designed to give you control, demonstrate professionalism, and build a defensible record.
Step 1: The Foundation – Your Intercompany Agreement

Your first and most powerful tool is a formal contract between your two companies: the **Intercompany Agreement (IA)**. Think of this not as a bureaucratic chore, but as the constitution for your global business. It proves to tax authorities that your internal dealings are deliberate, professional, and governed by a clear commercial framework.
This document formalizes the terms of engagement before any work is done. It should be the single source of truth for how your entities collaborate.
**Your "Contract with Yourself" Checklist**

header: Why It Matters (And What to Write)
rows: The full legal names and registered addresses of both your companies.

Establishes the distinct legal identity of each entity. **Example:** *"This Agreement is between YourName LLC, a Delaware limited liability company, and YourName Consulting Ltd., a private limited company registered in England and Wales."*
A specific description of the work one company will perform for the other.

Vague terms like "consulting" are a red flag. Be precise to prove the service has real commercial value. **Example:** *"YourName LLC will provide market research, competitive analysis, and strategic brand positioning services to YourName Consulting Ltd. on a project basis."*
The formula for calculating the price. This is the heart of your agreement.

Directly addresses the arm's length principle. You must show your pricing is what you would charge an unrelated client. **Example:** *"Services will be billed at an hourly rate of $175 USD, which reflects the fair market rate for equivalent services provided to third-party clients."* or *"Services will be billed on a cost-plus basis, calculated as total direct and indirect costs incurred plus a markup of 10%."*
The practical details of how and when invoices will be issued and paid.

Demonstrates a professional, arm's length commercial relationship. **Example:** *"Invoices will be issued monthly and are payable within 30 days via international wire transfer to the account specified on the invoice."*

body.content: Step 2: The Execution – Invoicing and Payments

An agreement is a statement of intent; the invoice and payment are the tangible proof. This step is about bringing your IA to life through consistent, professional execution, creating a clean paper trail that any tax authority can follow.
Here’s how to translate your agreement into action across common scenarios:
**Scenario 1: The Consultant**
Your US LLC develops a marketing strategy, but the client contract is with your UK Ltd. The UK Ltd is essentially "hiring" the US LLC for the strategic work.

items: **Action:** Your US LLC must issue a formal invoice to your UK Ltd for the strategy at the arm's length rate.
**Invoice Detail:** Instead of "Consulting," state *"Development of Q3 Marketing Strategy for Client X, as per Intercompany Agreement."*

**Pricing:** If you would charge a third-party client $5,000 for that strategy, you invoice your UK Ltd for $5,000. This creates a legitimate expense for the UK company and taxable revenue for the US company.

body.content: **Scenario 2: The Developer**
Your US entity owns the intellectual property (IP) for a piece of software. Your European entity licenses it to serve local clients.

items: **Action:** The European company must pay a fair market royalty or license fee to the US entity that owns the IP.
**Invoice Detail:** The invoice from your US entity could be for a *"Quarterly Software License Fee for 'Product Z' Usage in the EU Territory."*

**Pricing:** A common method is to base the royalty on a percentage of the revenue the European company earns using the software. Researching comparable industry license agreements is key to setting a defensible price.

body.content: **Scenario 3: Shared Costs**
You pay for a $1,000 software subscription through your US LLC, but you use it 60% for US projects and 40% for UK projects.

items: **Action:** You must allocate this cost fairly. The UK company benefited and must shoulder its portion of the expense.
**Invoice Detail:** Your US LLC should invoice your UK Ltd for *"Reimbursement of Pro-Rata Share of Software Subscription for FY 2025."*
**Pricing:** The calculation must be logical. Invoice the UK Ltd for $400 (40% of the $1,000 cost), reflecting its share of the benefit.

body.content: Step 3: The Defense – Your Contemporaneous Documentation

Many entrepreneurs fear they need a $20,000 formal transfer pricing study. For a solo-operated business, this is usually unnecessary. Your goal is to create **contemporaneous documentation**—a simple, organized file you build in real-time to prove you’ve made a reasonable, good-faith effort to comply. This file is your first and best line of defense.
**Your Compliance File Checklist:**

items: **Your Signed Intercompany Agreement:** The foundation of your defense, proving a formal commercial relationship exists.
**Copies of All Intercompany Invoices:** The record of each transaction executed under the terms of your agreement.
**Proof of Payment:** Bank statements from both accounts showing the money actually moved. An invoice alone is not enough.
**A One-Page Pricing Memo:** The capstone of your file. This is a short document explaining *how* you arrived at your price, grounding it in the arm's length principle.

body.content: Your memo can be as simple as this:

rows: "The fee for strategic consulting services is set at $175/hour. This rate is identical to the rate charged to our three largest third-party clients (Client A, Client B, Client C) for the same services during this fiscal year. This method was chosen to directly reflect the fair market value and adhere to the arm's length principle."
"This pricing will be reviewed annually to ensure it remains aligned with market rates."

body.content: Spending a few hours to assemble and maintain this file is the highest-ROI administrative task you can perform.
Frequently Asked Questions
**Do I need transfer pricing if I'm the only employee?**

Yes, absolutely. The rules apply to transactions between legally separate entities, regardless of employee count. When you formed two companies, you created two distinct legal "persons." The law requires them to transact with each other as if they were unrelated to maintain the integrity of your liability shield.
**What is a simple example of transfer pricing for a consultant?**

Your US LLC holds your brand and intellectual property. Your UK Ltd signs a client in London. The UK Ltd, which receives the client payment, must pay the US LLC a fee (e.g., a percentage of revenue) for the right to use its brand and methodologies to service that client. This payment is the transfer price, documented with an invoice from the US to the UK entity.
**How do I document transfer pricing without a $20,000 study?**

You build the "good-enough" compliance file detailed in Step 3. For a solo-run business, the priority is creating contemporaneous documentation—evidence that you established a fair price *at the time of the transaction*. Your self-built file containing the agreement, invoices, payment proofs, and a pricing memo achieves this.
**What is the arm's length principle in simple terms?**

It means the price for a transaction between your own companies must be the same as it would be if they were completely unrelated. The test is: "What would I pay an independent third party for this service?" That price ensures profits are taxed fairly in the countries where the value was actually created.
**What is an intercompany agreement?**

It is a formal contract between your two related companies that defines the commercial relationship. It outlines the specific services, the pricing methodology, and payment terms. Having this contract proves your internal dealings are not arbitrary but are governed by a clear, pre-defined framework—a critical element of robust compliance.
From Anxiety to Asset

Transfer pricing does not have to be a source of fear. By implementing this straightforward framework, you transform a compliance task into a strategic asset. You are not just satisfying tax authorities; you are validating the integrity of your global structure, sharpening your operational discipline, and building a more resilient enterprise.

You made the intelligent choice to build a business for global reach. Honoring that structure with professional diligence is the final step. This playbook provides the clarity and control to manage your international operations with the same expertise you bring to your clients.
